title: Developer-Led Growth
compiled_truth:
Developer-led growth (DLG) is the thesis that infrastructure companies can build durable revenue by acquiring individual developers as users first, then converting their organizations into paying customers later. The motion is bottom-up: a single engineer discovers a tool, integrates it into a side project or internal prototype, and gradually pulls it into production workloads. By the time a sales team engages the enterprise, the tool is already load-bearing. This is the playbook that matters most for deterministic data infrastructure, where the buyer is almost always a technical practitioner before they're a budget holder.

At Anchor Codec, we've watched this pattern play out with two portfolio-adjacent companies in particular. [Lattice Data](companies/lattice-data) built their initial user base almost entirely through open-source SDKs and a generous free tier. Engineers adopted the tooling for personal projects, brought it into staging environments, and by the time procurement got involved, Lattice was processing real traffic. Their first six enterprise contracts all originated from individual developer accounts — zero outbound. [Nexus Relay](companies/nexus-relay) followed a slightly different arc: they seeded adoption through developer advocates and conference workshops, then layered on a self-serve dashboard that made it trivial to upgrade. Both companies demonstrate that DLG isn't a single tactic; it's a spectrum of strategies unified by the same principle — reduce friction for the individual, and the organization follows.

[Skyler Okonkwo](people/skyler-okonkwo) has been one of the most vocal internal advocates for structuring our go-to-market around DLG principles. Her argument is straightforward: if a developer can't get value from our single-pass primitives within 15 minutes, the enterprise motion will never work because there's no organic pull. [Kael Petrov](people/kael-petrov) pushes back on this sometimes — he worries about premature optimization of the free tier at the expense of enterprise feature depth — but even he acknowledges that our strongest pipeline deals in 2027 came from bottom-up adoption.

The risks of DLG are real. You can accumulate thousands of free users who never convert. You can under-invest in sales infrastructure because growth "feels" organic. And you can mistake developer enthusiasm for willingness to pay. The thesis only works if there's a clear value inflection — a moment where the individual's usage naturally bumps into a team or compliance boundary that triggers a paid tier. Without that wedge, you're just running a popular open-source project with no business model.

For infrastucture companies specifically, DLG has one structural advantage: the switching cost is high once a primitive is embedded in a data pipeline. That stickiness is the moat.
timeline:
- **2026-03-14** | [Skyler Okonkwo](people/skyler-okonkwo) circulates internal memo titled "Why We Should Ship a Free Tier Before Hiring a Single AE" — sparks weeks of debate across engineering and go-to-market teams.
- **2026-06-22** | First public developer sandbox goes live. 340 signups in the first 48 hours, mostly from Hacker News traffic.
- **2026-09-10** | [Lattice Data](companies/lattice-data) shares case study at InfraCon showing how 82% of their enterprise pipeline originated from individual developer accounts. Team studies it closely.
- **2026-12-01** | [Kael Petrov](people/kael-petrov) presents counterpoint analysis at quarterly offsite: free-tier users consuming significant compute but conversion rate sitting at 1.4%.
- **2027-02-18** | Usage-based pricing tier introduced, creating a natural upgrade trigger when developers exceed 50k daily operations.
- **2027-05-30** | [Nexus Relay](companies/nexus-relay) announces $28M Series B, explicitly citing developer-led growth metrics as the core narrative to investors.
- **2027-08-14** | First enterprise deal ($220k ACV) traces directly back to a single engineer's free-tier account created 11 months earlier. Internal Slack erupts.
- **2027-11-03** | DLG metrics dashboard shipped internally — tracks individual-to-team expansion, time-to-first-paid-event, and organic referral chains.
- **2028-01-22** | [Skyler Okonkwo](people/skyler-okonkwo) and [Kael Petrov](people/kael-petrov) co-author blog post "Bottom-Up Is Not Free: What DLG Actually Costs" — becomes most-shared piece on the company blog.
- **2028-04-09** | Conversion rate hits 4.7% after introduction of team-collaboration features gated behind paid tier. Board formally adopts DLG as primary GTM framework.

_facts.name: developer-led growth
_facts.description: Bottom-up adoption via individual developers before enterprise sales motion kicks in
